content:
12 min read
1 hour ago

--

This article introduces Spec-Driven Development and provides a hands-on experience building real projects with SDD and Claude Code.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Spec-Driven Development (SDD) is a software development methodology in which structured specifications are authored before any code is generated.
Why SDD Matters?
AI agents are prone to making mistakes. SDD helps us steer the agent in the right direction.
In SDD, the person writing the spec plays the role of an architect, defines concrete specs and reviews them, while the agent plays the role of a developer who follows the requirements and implements.
Four phases of SDD
‣ Specify
This is where you specify the base information in a structured, natural-language format for the feature you’re building — requirements, business scenarios, edge cases, acceptance criteria, implementation details, designs, data models, additional objectives, and, of course, any open questions the agent may have for you.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Sample specification fileThis is the most important step, so be as detailed as possible. This step will probably involve back-and-forth discussion with the agent on how to put everything together. That’s why it’s important to write the Open questions sections so that the agent can ask you about things that it doesn’t quite understand or needs confirmation.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Claude asked me questions about the requirementReply to the questions in the same markdown file, then ask Claude to review the answers.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Once you agree on implementation, ask Claude to generate an execution plan that includes the details of what you’ve agreed on.
‣ Plan
The plan will include detailed steps for implementing the requested feature. This file will be written by Claude.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Execution plan generated by Claude‣ Implement
You’ve reviewed the plan and decided to proceed. Pass the specification to the AI agent to generate code and tests that match the exact parameters (execution plan).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

‣ Validate
Verify the generated output against the established acceptance criteria and verification criteria. This can be either a manual review of the work or a code review using GitHub Spec Kit.

To see how this works in action, we’re going to use Claude to build two different projects:
#1 Responsive Web UI
Provided only an image and a base project structure, Claude will analyze and create a fully responsive static web UI.
#2 Database migrations for an existing Node.js backend
We’ll put Claude into the existing Node.js backend that stores data in JSON files. We’ll write detailed instructions to make a migration, including:

• REST API changes,

• Database migration script,

• PostgreSQL Docker image,

• API Error Handling,

• Health checks,

• Documentation,

• API test cases

#1 Building Responsive Web UI
You can follow along from here. I’ve just created a base project structure in the src and spec directories. The spec directory contains two images for the UI and a template markdown file as a starting point for Claude.
‣ Specify phase
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Contents of the markdown file# Feature: Profile page## Problem / Motivation
Create a profile page from profile-page.png.
## Goals
Analyze the spec/profile-page.png file. Create an EXECUTION.md file with implementation steps on how to recreate the page in this project. Suggest colors, margins, paddings and themes from the image analyzed.
## Non-goals
Don't worry about the profile picture or the logo in the navbar. They can stay empty.
## Open questions
Now I’m going to run Claude from the project root in the terminal and ask it to analyze the spec directory.
Hey Claude. I’ve created the /spec folder. Analyze it and ask any questions you may have in the template.md
Claude did his magic, asked for permission to edit the TEMPLATE.md file, and asked me a few questions:
## Open questions1. **Interactivity**: Should any buttons (Edit Profile, Settings, Continue Quest, Start Quest, Your All Merodement) have click behavior, or are they purely decorative for now?
2. **Data source**: Should the stats and quest data (XP, streak count, quest names, progress %) be hardcoded in HTML, or driven by a JavaScript data object/array so they're easy to swap?
3. **Font**: The design uses a stylized gaming font for headings. Should I use a specific Google Font (e.g. Orbitron, Rajdhani) to match, or is any clean sans-serif acceptable?
4. **Responsive layout**: Should the page adapt to mobile/tablet screen sizes, or is desktop-only sufficient?
5. **Animations/effects**: Are hover glows on cards, animated progress bars, or pulsing neon borders desired, or keep it static?
I replied to these questions in the same file and asked it to proceed.
‣ Plan phase
After we agreed on the specification, Claude created a step-by-step plan for building the website based on the provided images and requirements (template.md).
Everything is listed in a newly created execution.md file. This is a summary. You can find the full file on GitHub.
# EXECUTION.md — Profile Page Implementation## Reference Files
- Desktop: `spec/profile-page.png`
- Mobile: `spec/mobile-profile-page.png`
## 1. Design Tokens (CSS Variables)
...
## 2. Typography
...
## 3. HTML Structure
...
## 4. Component Implementation
...
### 4.1 Navbar
...
### 4.2 Profile Header Card
...
### 4.3 Current Quest Card
...
### 4.4 Stats Row
...
### 4.5 Recommended Quests
...
### 4.6 Completed Quests
...
### 4.7 Footer
...
## 5. Responsive Breakpoints
...
## 6. Implementation Order
I was happy with it, so I prompted it to proceed.
I like the execution.md. Please implement the plan in the src directory
‣ Implementation phase
Claude started doing what it does best: generating code based on the execution file.
Five minutes later, we have a working website preview.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

‣ Validation phase
Now it’s time to verify the work. I definitely recommend checking the generated code, and if there is anything you'd like fixed, prompt Claude to do so.
Of course, I had to check the mobile view and compare it to the original. It looks pretty good, but the middle streak section is missing, progress-bar color, text color, button colors, checkmarks, and border width are wrong.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Failed Successfully!I definitely should have paid more attention to reviewing the execution.md file. As I said, you can prompt Claude to fix it, but since this is a very simple demo, I’ll skip. You can find the changes here.
If you’re wondering how much this costs to implement, here’s a full breakdown:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

# 2 Database migrations: Node.js REST API
Now we get to the real deal. I added Claude to the existing Express.js project that stores data in JSON files and asked it to migrate the data to a PostgreSQL database. This is the starting point.
Here is the template file:
# Feature: Migrate Character Storage to PostgreSQL## Problem / Motivation
Saving Characters to the database.
## Goals
Move the data from the db/data.json file into the actual database. We'll use PostgreSQL.
For this, we need to add PostgreSQL image into our Docker compose file.
## Non-goals
- Database schema migrations beyond the Character model
- Adding new Character fields
## API design
<irrelevant>
## Test cases
Include sample Postman requests for each endpoint on Character router. Add one case for the success and one for the error.
Put tests in the specs/real_db/TESTS.md directory.
## Diagrams
Create sequence diagram for each endpoint on Character router. Use Mermaid.js.
Put diagrams in the specs/real_db/DIAGRAMS.md directory.
## Data models
Character model:
```js
class Character {
id: number;
name: string;
profession: string;
race: string;
dateInserted: Date;
dateUpdated: Date;
}
```
- Make sure to autoincrement "id" by one on each insert.
- Make sure to set appropriate constraints on the string fields (e.g., nvarchar(100))
- Columns "dateInserted" and "dateUpdated" will default to the current date and time upon inserting a record.
- After updating the record, "dateUpdated" will be set to the date of the update, while the "dateInserted" will remain unchanged.
## Events
<irrelevant>
## Implementation plan
1. Set up a PostgreSQL docker image in the docker-compose.yml file
2. Install the Prisma ORM and the Node.js driven for PGSQL
3. Set up the Prisma ORM in the src/db folder (db.js)
4. Wire up the src/db/db.js file in the app.js file
5. Extend the health check readyness route (src/health/) to also include checking out database connection as part of the readyness test
6. Updated characters.repository.js. Replace inserts and queries for data.json (FileSystem) with the database queries for Prisma ORM
7. Wrap database operations with the try-catch block
## Open questions
I once again asked Claude to review the template file and add the open questions. The first few questions were strictly database-related.
**Claude:** Worth deciding actual lengths for `name`/`profession`/`race` — or
just use `TEXT` and skip arbitrary length limits unless there's
a real constraint.**Me:** Let's use a constraint VARCHAR(50).
**Claude:** `id` auto-increment: current `data.json` approach.
With Postgres we'd use a `SERIAL`/`IDENTITY` column
(Prisma: `@id @default(autoincrement())`). Should existing IDs
from `data.json` be preserved on migration, or can they be reassigned? **Me:** Let's preserve it if possible.
**Claude**: Do we need a one-time seed script that reads
`src/db/data.json` and inserts those rows into Postgres
(with `dateInserted` defaulted to "now" for all of them),
or is starting with an empty table fine?**Me:** Let's create a migration script that reads from data.json file
and inserts data into the table.
Then we got to the interesting question about error handling.
**Claude:** ("wrap database operations in try-catch") -
where should errors surface? Currently the routers don't have a
generic error-handling pattern; this might be a good moment to add a
small Express error-handling middleware rather than scattering
try-catch + ad-hoc 500 responses across each route.**Me:** Can you generate me a snippet of what you have in mind?
Once I finished answering questions, I prompted Claude to review my answers.
You’ll find my replies in the Open questions section. Please go over them and let me know your thoughts.
Then we got to the second round of questions.
**Claude**: VARCHAR(50) — fine, all current values are well under that.
Going with `VARCHAR(50)` for `name`/`profession`/`race`.**Claude**: Preserving IDs via seed script — doable, but needs one extra
step: when you `INSERT` rows with explicit `id` values, Postgres's
autoincrement sequence doesn't know about them and will start from 1 again,
causing a collision on the next `createCharacter`.
I'll bake that into the seed script.**Me:** Can we just do this with the for loop?
If not, let's start over and do t your recommended way.
**Claude**: Yes — a `for` loop is exactly how the seed script inserts each row
(one`prisma.character.create({ data: { id, name, ... } })` per character,
preserving the `id` from `data.json`).
**Me:** OK
This time around, Claude got deeper into the error-handling mechanism I asked to elaborate on, and he did not disappoint:
**Claude:** Error-handling middleware — here's the shape I'm thinking of: // src/middleware/error.middleware.js
// Wrap async route handlers so rejected promises reach the error middleware
const asyncHandler = (fn) => (req, res, next) => {
fn(req, res, next).catch(next);
};
// Centralized error handler — last middleware in app.js
function errorHandler(err, req, res, next) {
console.error(err);
const status = err.status || 500;
res.status(status).send({
status,
message: status === 5
